speaker: interviewer
text: --with Lusia Haberfeld. Lusia, you were telling me the day that you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After being three months in Majdanek, and witnessing a terrible lot of atrocities and deaths and horror, they came. As I said to you, there were rumors that the Germans are losing the war and the-- that the-- the end is coming. And the Germans, as I said, were wearing white bands. And one day, a tank rolled into the camp. And the tank had a white star. And there was sitting somebody in a different uniform to the SS uniform. And we, the Jewish women-- because we knew the Germans. Everybody was saying look, it's-- it's a foreign tank. We've been liberated. It's a foreign tank. And we, the Jewish women in camp, said, no. We haven't been liberated. The Germans are just staging a show to see how we're going to react. Because that was just exactly what they would do. They were these kind of-- of people. But there were with us Ukrainian. And the Uk-- not Jewish women, Ukrainian. And those who came-- they were very tough and rough women. Ooh, were they tough and rough. I was with them in-- in Birkenau, too. And what they did-- I don't know how they found a twig, a twig without leaves, just an empty twig. I don't know where they found it. And they ran to the tank. And they handed in the twig to the man who was sitting in the tank. And he took the twig. And all of a sudden, we realize there's no shooting. There's nothing. We have been liberated. So everybody started running to the tank. And on the-- in the tank was sitting this English-- we were liberated by English army-- was sitting this English very high ranking officer. Later-- he was Jewish. Later on, we found out he was Jewish. And next to him was sitting this [GERMAN], Kramer. They got Kramer, you see. He didn't manage to run away. They got him. And that was our liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know-- our liberation was joyful and very, very sad. I can't describe to you-- I can't say that it was something that we celebrated, because we didn't. We were absolutely overwhelmed. We didn't know, you know, who we were. And all of a sudden, we started thinking, what happened to you know, my father, my brother? My brother-- we knew. We had a feeling. We knew what they were doing to-- after what happened to me in Majdanek, where they put me, I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, and it was joyful and it was sad, at the same time. I don't remember rejoicing. Really, I don't. Although I felt like a-- a house has been lifted from my shoulders, you know-- this kind of feeling of relief. It was a kind of relief. But yet, it wasn't altogether joyful. And a lot of people have died after the liberation-- a lot of prisoners. Oh, I shouldn't be calling them prisoners. It is-- it's idiotic. You know, they died, because the English, in their kindness, the army, in their kindness, saw those-- those starved skeletons. And they started feeding them food. And their stomachs couldn't take them. And they died of dysentery-- a lot of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we're liberated, and we went out, we saw a sight that I also will never forget. We saw a pile of bodies that reached at least to the first floor. By the pile are thousands, and thousands, and thousands of dead bodies, piled on top of each other. And they got the SS people to clean it up, the English. And we were standing there. And one woman-- I remember, I was envying her, her-- her courage that she did it. She went to this SS woman and told her to take off her boots. And she took off her boots. And she put on her boots. And I thought, how marvelous. How wonderful, you know? She already felt she's-- she's been liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my mother, you know, I said, remember one thing, I said to my mother. That in the war-- it wasn't the end of the war yet. Because we were liberated on the 15th of April. And the war finished on the 8th of May. So I said to my mother, remember that in the war, armies retreat sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to my mother, what will happen if the English army will retreat and the Germans will come back. After I told her that, we never slept anymore. We were getting ready to follow the English army, you know. Because we're scared if they retreat, we'll follow them. We will never stay, you know, for some more sufferings. And that's about it. I have forgotten a lot of things. But this is about it. What happened to you after the liberation, after the war finished? Did you go into a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberation, yes, we were in a camp. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen. And we were given a room. Oh, we had already different-- altogether different conditions, you know. We were human beings again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need medical treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- not at that stage. I didn't need any medical treatment. With me, everything started coming later, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was in-- how was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother also didn't need any med-- she was sick in the camp. I was sick in camp a lot. We were sick a lot in camp, my mother and I. But after the liberation, she was OK. She was still a young woman, you know? She was in her 30s or perhaps early 40s. She was still a young woman. And she was OK. And we-- we stayed in this-- you know, they put us into barracks. And we got a room between four girls, so my mother and I and two other ladies, which we became very friendly with. And one day, you know who came to visit us? Ben-Gurion. Ben-Gurion came to the-- to the block. And I spoke to him. And he spoke to me in Polish, because I could not speak Yiddish at the time yet. And of course, I couldn't speak Hebrew. And he spoke to me in Polish. And he said to me-- he asked me my name, and everything, and what I went through. And he said to me, come. He said, come. We need you. Come to-- it wasn't Israel yet. It was Palestine. Come to Palestine. We need you. And he spoke to me quite a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want--

speaker: interviewee
text: --long time. Because I was the youngest in the-- in the block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very much. I wanted to go very much, yes. And when-- after we're liberated, there was a-- an army rabbi from England called [? Vilensky. ?] And he wanted to adopt me after the war and give me a proper education and everything, and take me to London. But of course, my mother said, I can't do that. Just think, I've already lost my son my husband. I've got nobody else. And she wouldn't let me go. And I didn't mention to you that in Warsaw ghetto, I learned English. When I had those lessons, there was a woman that spoke-- it was said it was like that. She married an English Jew, before the war, and went to live in England. And she came, shortly before they broke out, to visit her mother, back to Poland. And she wasn't an English citizen yet. And when they broke out-- war broke out, she couldn't get out. And when the Germans came, she couldn't get out anymore. And she was stuck in Warsaw. And our teacher got her-- because she had nothing to live from, either-- to teach us English. So on the lessons, we had English lessons as well. And after the liberation, the English army took over a laundry after the SS. And they needed to have personnel there. And they took the girls from camp to work for them. And they needed somebody to be an interpreter. So I volunteered, because when I started talking to the English, all the words, every thing I learned, started coming back. So while I was in-- in the-- in those three camps, with so many different girls from different nationalities, I learned to speak Czechoslovakian and Slovakian. And I understood a lot of Hungarian-- not everything, but the gist of it. And German I learned to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- and Polish, of course, I spoke. So I was translating to the English army, to the-- you know, from the personnel, in English, whatever that girls wanted, and whatever the soldiers wanted to-- from the girls to do for them. I was the interpretor. Would you believe it? How long did you stay in Bergen-Belsen DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we were-- got to work in this laundry, they gave us-- oh [INAUDIBLE]. They gave us a villa, after the SS. And they put six or eight girls into the villa, only the girls that were working for the English. And so we had a very nice time there. And one day-- and that was before we got to the villa, or maybe it was in the villa-- a man came and said, are there any women here from Lodz. And my mother said, yes, I'm from Lodz. And another woman was there. And he said, look, I come from Switzerland. And we have a lot of men. But we have no ladies. And we would like to bring some ladies to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we wanted really, very badly to get out of the DP camp, my mother and I. We wanted to have a normal life, you know? So my mother took the address, what he gave her. And she went, with another lady. And it turned out not to be Switzerland, but it turned out to be a little township on the Swiss border, which just called Konstanz. And my mother and I went to Konstanz. She went away six weeks. And I was in the meantime in Bergen-Belsen. I made friends with girls. And I learned more English. I went to English lessons. And from Israel-- or Palestine, at the time-- from Israel-- look, I I'll call it Israel from now on. And from Israel, they came teachers. And they made a school. And I went to that school while I was in Bergen-Belsen DP camp. And I learned, for a few months there-- I went to school and they was teaching us Hebrew. And they was teaching us a lot of things. And we had lunch every day. And from a little, skinny girl, I became a very fat, fat girl. Because we had lunch every day. And they used to put on the table whole plates of chocolate. Would you believe it? I didn't see it for so many years. And we're all eating ourselves crazy. And I-- we all became very, very fat in Bergen-Belsen after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, my-- I got out of the school, because my mother took me to Konstanz. And when I arrived in Konstanz, there were nobody my age there. And they were all people my mother's age. And my mother wanted to put me to German school. And I-- I'm very sorry I didn't go. But I have had-- I have some very high principles. When I was younger, I used to have high principle. And they really worked against me. Later on, as I found out-- because I wanted-- my mother wanted me to go to school. I said, no way am I going to sit on a bench with a German child, whose father might have killed my father. And I didn't want to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we found out that 80 kilometers away there was a beautiful place, also on Swiss border, bordering with Schaffhausen, where there was a lot of sanatorium before the war. It was a healing place for Jewish people. Jewish people had sanatorium. And the Germans have given the sanatoriums to the people who were survivors. And they made a lot of kibbutzim there. And there were a lot of young people who survived. And some were in Russia during the war, you know. Some were hidden. And they were in kibbutz. And my mother and I decided that it's no good for me to be with grownups in Konstanz. And I took a train. And I went to Gailingen. That was the name of the place. And I was on a kibbutz. And this was the most wonderful time of my life. Because I made a lot of friends. And we became very close to each other. And I learned a lot in the kibbutz. Who was supporting you on this kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the money come from in this kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The money came from-- oh, I forgot the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a name of an organization in Munich. It was a central organization. I forgot the name. And they supported all the kibbutzim. Now, some kibbutzim were political affiliated. Ours was not. Ours was only for young people. It was called [NON-ENGLISH], which means [INAUDIBLE]. Only young [NON-ENGLISH] were there, you know. Not [NON-ENGLISH]-- maybe [NON-ENGLISH]. I think it was called [NON-ENGLISH], that place, the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was with you, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. My mother was in Konstanz. And my mother-- I told you. We decided that I don't want to go to school. I cannot be there with the grown ups. I have to go where there are young-- I didn't want to be there. I wanted to go where there are young people. I wanted to learn. And the next best thing to a school was a kibbutz. Because we did have lessons, Hebrew lessons, history lessons. And we had a wonderful young people there, wonderful-- such intelligent young people, you have no idea, with our own band, our own orchestra. The people were playing on instruments. And I had a wonderful time there. And my mother came to visit me pretty often. And I used to go visit my mother. And while I was in the kibbutz, my mother met my stepfather. And she came to ask me for permission whether she can marry him-- I mean, she would have married him, anyhow. But it was very sweet of her to do that. And I was very upset about it-- very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then, at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1945, I would have been, what-- 14, 15 years old already. It was '46, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you on the kibbutz for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 18 months, two years, like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your aims at the time? Were they to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Oh, yes. Oh, yes. In the meantime also, we heard from my cousin, my mother's niece, you know. She survived camp in Czechoslovakia. And she's the one that brought the picture from my brother that she found in the-- in our flat, after the war. And we went to visit her. They were living in an American zone, in [INAUDIBLE], I think. But going back to the kibbutz, yes, we were learning to-- our aim was to go to the-- to Israel. And of course, in that time, everybody was going with [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the [NON-ENGLISH] went through our village. We had the-- oh, I forgot their name. Used to remember their name. I forgot their name. They had a army of people who used to smuggle people into our village. The kibbutzim-- we had three, four kibbutzim-- absorbed them for a few days, or a week, or two weeks-- whatever was necessary. And then they smuggled them to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why didn't you go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our kibbutz went [NON-ENGLISH] to Israel. And my mother wouldn't let me. She said, I'm not going to part with you now. I have nobody else. I'm not going to part with you. And going back to my mother, you know, she said to me-- she's going to get married with the-- she has my permission. And at-- in the beginning, I said, no, you don't have my permission. I don't want you to get married. But she said to me, Lusia, you are a girl, a young girl. You are-- whatever-- 15, 14, 16. I didn't remember what year it was exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day she said to me, you are going to get married. And you're going to leave me. And I'll be on-- on my own. And I'll be a very lonely woman. Let me get married so I won't be on my own. So I could see that she's right, you know? And I said, OK, you get married. But I never went to the wedding. And my mother married this man. And she wouldn't let me go with Aliya Bet to Israel. But all my friends went. Because she said she can't part with me, which I can understand. And then I went to live in Konstanz, because Gailingen was gone. That was in 1947 or '48. No-- it must have been '47, because '48 already we had-- State of Israel was declared. So it was 1947, or maybe beginning of '47, they all went. And then I went to Konstanz. And I wasn't so very happy there, because I didn't have the company anymore, you know, and they were all families. There was only one single girl that I kept friendly with. Everybody else was marriage, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we stayed there. And I get to know my-- I got to know my husband in Konstanz. He was there. He's a survivor himself. And he was liberated in French occupation zone. And Konstanz was occupied by French. And I met my husband there. And we got married in-- at the end of 1949. In October, 1949 we got married. And soon after, we left for Australia. We arrived in Australia in March, 5th of March, 1950. So I think we must have left-- we were a couple of weeks in Italy, waiting for the boats. We paid our own passage. Because the [INAUDIBLE] wouldn't pay for us, because we lived on the French zone. So because we paid for our own passage, I went to Zurich, in Switzerland. And I got the tickets on the boat. And because of that, we had a cabin to ourselves. We didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How could you afford it, to get to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, somehow my husband had the money. We could afford. That was our-- all the money we had, you know? And my mother did the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to go to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to go to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was like that. We wanted to get out of Germany, very badly. I just couldn't take the language. I-- I had such a hatred for the people, you know, at the time. I didn't want to stay there. And we-- that is, my mother tried to get into three countries-- Israel-- Palestine, because in Israel she could go-- Palestine, United States, and Australia-- Australia for the reasons that she had a friend, before the war, who lived in Australia for a while, long before the war probably. And he loved Australia so much. He was living-- and he said, it's a wonderful country. It's a a country of future. It's a country that you can do well. But he, himself, came back. Because he couldn't take the climate. And my mother always remembered that. And she said that she arranged for the papers to Australia, too, you see, because we were refugees. The minute the war finished, my mother and I renounced our polish citizenship. We became stateless. We didn't want to be-- because of what the Poles did to the Jewish people. We were going to go back to Poland and have a look what happened to our home-- you know, home. And we already had a guide to go. But they made a pogrom in Kielce just as we were going to go. And I said to my mother, we didn't go through this hell to be killed in Poland now. So we didn't go. We never back-- went back to Poland. But we renounced our Polish citizenship and we became stateless. And as stateless people, were asked to be let in, in other countries. And out of these three countries, my mother said, wherever the papers would come first, that is our fate to go. And the papers came first to Australia. So we went to Australia. We came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived-- we arrived in 5th of March, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In which town, which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Melbourne. We arrived in Melbourne. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any help getting settled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- how did you decide where to live--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any-- I hated it so much, I can't even tell you. I really hated it. First of all, I couldn't stand the climate. You know, we arrived in March. And the-- I wasn't used to it, that the wind was cold in your back, and the sun was hot, you know? And I was always sick. I forever catching colds, you know. And I was pregnant. I got pregnant on the way to Australia. And I was sick all the time. And we rented a room. It was terrible what they did to us. They took five pounds for an empty room from us, in Bentley. The people who made permits for my husband were living in Bentley. And we came to Bentley to this empty room. And it was horrible there. And five pounds-- we could have-- we couldn't afford it. And my husband straight away started looking for a job. And this time, there were plenty of jobs. So we were two or three weeks home, getting accustomed a little bit. And then he got a job and I got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you work as?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget it, because I've got two left hands. I'm not very good with my hands. And the job I got was in a clothing factory. They were making men's suits. And in those days, really, people-- you couldn't get people enough to work, enough people to work. They would take anybody to work, even a person like me, you know? And he wanted-- the boss put me on a machine and to-- to put sleeves together, to sew sleeves together. And I went like this, you know, because I didn't know how to do it. So he gave me a little gadget. And I couldn't go wrong. I made straight-- you know, the sleeves, the seam was straight. And I'm very fast. And I-- I made a lot of sleeves for him. But I was pregnant, as I said. And downstairs was a fish and chips shop, and a pie shop. And I was all the time nauseated, you know. And although I worked there two or three months, I-- I couldn't work there. Because I was sick. And the girls who were working there, they didn't know what happened to me. They didn't know I was pregnant. I didn't know myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, oh, you're such a young girl. And you're so sick, you know? I had to give up the job because I couldn't stand the smell. And the boss-- he just couldn't believe that I am giving up the job. He thought somebody gave me a better offer. And he just couldn't-- till I, you know, I said, look, nobody gave you-- and-- and I got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when I was in Birkenau, I had-- you asked me whether I was sick. I had a few attacks on my stomach, terrible attacks. And I had pain. After the war, they realized that I had gallstones. But my first attacks were in Birkenau. And the doctor who examined us-- prior to go to Australia, we had to go through a medical commission. He said to me, when you will be pregnant, you will be very, very sick with this gallstones. That was a Hungarian doctor. And sure enough, when I arrived here, and I gave up my job, I had such terrible attacks. In gallbladder, the stones went out of the duct-- out of the back, into the duct. And I got jaundice. And I was so sick they didn't know what to do with me. And my husband knew some people here. They were nice. They got us very good doctors, you know. And they made consultations, three very good doctors. And they said that I have to be operated on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, I was already six or seven months pregnant. And I said, what's going to happen to the baby? So they said, they can't guarantee the baby. So I said, then forget it. I'm not getting operated. They said, you're taking a terrible risk with your life. So I said, so be it, you know. And for the rest of my pregnancy, I was on morphine. I was very, very sick. And Michael was born. And when he was two years old, I was operated on my gallstones. And I was operated by-- I don't know whether you ever heard of him-- Edward Dunlop. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's a very well-known man in Australia, because he was a prisoner of war, a Japanese prisoner of war. And he was doing operation on Australian soldiers without any anesthetic. And he suffered through similar things that I did. You know, he was a very big man. There is a stamp in his honor, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to Australia. And I hated every minute of it. And you had a second son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was later, yeah. My second son, Leslie, was born in 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had things changed by then? Did you like Australia better?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked Australia better. And-- but still, I felt very lonely here-- terribly lonely. Because I wasn't used to houses. We all lived in houses. Before, we all lived in apartments. And then my mother, of course, was married. And she went her own way. And I went my way. And my husband was working very hard. And we got a flat. And I took a lodger, because we couldn't afford to pay rent. And I felt very lonely. But I was starting to get acclimatized, you know. It takes time.
